topics.title: The Bible, Tradition, and Church Authority
topics.question: Is the Bible alone sufficient as the rule of faith, or do we need Sacred Tradition and the Church's teaching authority?
topics.answer: The Bible itself teaches that Scripture alone is not sufficient as the sole rule of faith. The Bible is the inspired Word of God (2 Tim 3:16), but it requires the Church's authority to determine which books belong to it, which translations are accurate, and how to interpret it correctly. St. Peter warns that Scripture is not a matter of private interpretation (2 Pet 1:20). Paul commands us to hold fast to the traditions he passed on orally and in writing (2 Thes 2:15). Christ founded a Church with authority to teach, not just a book (Mat 28:18-20). The Church preceded the written New Testament — the earliest Christians had only the oral apostolic tradition. Three questions no book can answer for itself: (1) Which books belong in the Bible? (2) Which translation is accurate? (3) What do disputed passages mean? The Catholic Church, guided by the Holy Spirit, answers all three through its teaching authority (Magisterium). Without the Church, there would be no Bible.

scripture.text: Therefore, brothers, stand firm and hold fast to the traditions that you were taught, either by an oral statement or by a letter of ours.
And so I say to you, you are Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church, and the gates of the netherworld shall not prevail against it.
But if I should be delayed, you should know how to behave in the household of God, which is the church of the living God, the pillar and foundation of truth.

Know this first of all, that there is no prophecy of scripture that is a matter of personal interpretation, for no prophecy ever came through human will; but rather human beings moved by the holy Spirit spoke under the influence of God.

churchFathers.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
churchFathers.quote: I would not believe in the Gospel, had not the authority of the Catholic Church already moved me.

churchFathers.author: St. Irenaeus of Lyon
churchFathers.quote: When there is a dispute about questions of some importance, should we not have recourse to the oldest Churches, the ones where the Apostles lived, and learn from them the definitive and clear answer to the problem at issue?

topics.title: The True Church of Christ
topics.question: Among all Christian denominations, how do we identify the true Church founded by Jesus Christ?
topics.answer: Christ founded one Church, not many (Eph 4:4-5). He promised that this Church would endure until the end of time and that the gates of hell would not prevail against it (Mat 16:18). The true Church can be identified by four marks: it is One (unified in faith, sacraments, and leadership), Holy (set apart by God, producing saints), Catholic (universal, for all people of all ages), and Apostolic (tracing its authority directly to the Apostles through unbroken succession). The Catholic Church alone can demonstrate all four marks throughout history. No denomination founded by a human being in the 16th century or later can claim apostolic continuity. The Catholic Church traces its founding directly to Christ through Peter and the Apostles. There is no other Church that can claim an unbroken line of succession from the Apostles to the present day.

scripture.text: And so I say to you, you are Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church, and the gates of the netherworld shall not prevail against it. I will give you the keys to the kingdom of heaven. Whatever you bind on earth shall be bound in heaven; and whatever you loose on earth shall be loosed in heaven.
One body and one Spirit, as you were also called to the one hope of your call; one Lord, one faith, one baptism.

In the lifetime of those kings the God of heaven will set up a kingdom that shall never be destroyed or delivered up to another people; rather, it shall break in pieces all these kingdoms and put an end to them, and it shall stand forever.

churchFathers.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage

churchFathers.author: St. Ignatius of Antioch

topics.title: The Holy Trinity
topics.question: Where is the Trinity in the Bible, and how can one God be three Persons?
topics.answer: While the word 'Trinity' does not appear in the Bible, the doctrine is thoroughly biblical. God reveals himself as three distinct Persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — who share one divine nature. At Jesus' baptism, all three Persons are simultaneously present: the Father speaks from heaven, the Son is baptized, and the Holy Spirit descends as a dove (Mat 3:16-17). Jesus commands baptism 'in the name' (singular) of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Mat 28:19). The Son is called God in John 1:1; the Holy Spirit is called God in Acts 5:3-4. The mystery is that God is not three gods (that would be polytheism), nor is he one Person who plays three roles (that is the heresy of Modalism). Rather, there is one divine Being in three co-equal, co-eternal Persons. This truth, beyond full human comprehension, was revealed by God himself and has been the consistent faith of the Church from the beginning.

scripture.text: Go, therefore, and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the holy Spirit.
In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.

After Jesus was baptized, he came up from the water and behold, the heavens were opened and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and coming upon him. And a voice came from the heavens, saying, 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.'
The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the holy Spirit be with all of you.

churchFathers.author: St. Athanasius of Alexandria
churchFathers.quote: The Father is God, the Son is God, and the Holy Spirit is God, and yet there are not three Gods but one God.

churchFathers.author: Tertullian
churchFathers.quote: All the simple people who never consider what is due to the uniqueness of the Godhead, interpret it as if God were either diluted or multiplied, suppose that we are preaching three Gods.

topics.title: The Divinity of Jesus Christ
topics.question: Did Jesus claim to be God, and what evidence supports his divine nature?
topics.answer: Jesus clearly claimed to be God in multiple ways. He said 'Before Abraham came to be, I AM' (Jn 8:58) — using God's own name from Exodus 3:14. He claimed the power to forgive sins (Mk 2:5-7), which Jews rightly understood as a divine prerogative. He accepted worship (Mat 28:9, Jn 20:28). The Gospel of John opens by calling the Word (Jesus) 'God' (Jn 1:1). Paul declares that 'in him dwells the whole fullness of the deity bodily' (Col 2:9). The Letter to the Hebrews addresses Christ as 'God' (Heb 1:8). His resurrection from the dead is the ultimate confirmation of his divine nature (Rom 1:4). The early Church, those closest to the events, consistently worshipped Jesus as God — and were persecuted precisely for refusing to worship pagan gods while worshipping Christ.

scripture.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
Jesus said to them, 'Amen, amen, I say to you, before Abraham came to be, I AM.'
For in him dwells the whole fullness of the deity bodily.
But of the Son he says: 'Your throne, O God, stands forever and ever; and a righteous scepter is the scepter of your kingdom.'

churchFathers.author: St. Ignatius of Antioch
churchFathers.quote: There is one Physician who is possessed both of flesh and spirit; both made and not made; God existing in flesh; true life in death; both of Mary and of God.

topics.title: The Immaculate Conception of Mary
topics.question: Why do Catholics believe Mary was conceived without original sin, and where is this in Scripture?
topics.answer: The Immaculate Conception means that Mary, by a singular grace of God, was preserved from original sin from the moment of her conception. God prepared a pure vessel to bear His Son, just as the Ark of the Covenant — the dwelling place of God's Word — was made of pure gold. The angel Gabriel's greeting 'full of grace' (kecharitomene in Greek) is a perfect passive participle indicating a permanent, completed state of grace — not merely 'favored' at that moment. In Genesis 3:15, God foretells enmity between the serpent and the woman — total enmity requires that she be untouched by sin. Elizabeth exclaims that Mary is 'blessed among women' (Lk 1:42) — a Hebrew superlative meaning the most blessed of all women. Mary herself proclaims 'My spirit rejoices in God my savior' — she needed a savior too, but God saved her preemptively, like catching someone before they fall.

scripture.text: And coming to her, he said, 'Hail, favored one! The Lord is with you.'
I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and hers; he will strike at your head, while you strike at his heel.
Most blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb.

churchFathers.author: St. Ephrem the Syrian
churchFathers.quote: You alone and your Mother are more beautiful than anything. For on you, O Lord, there is no flaw, and in your Mother there is no stain.

topics.title: The Perpetual Virginity of Mary
topics.question: Did Mary remain a virgin after the birth of Jesus, and who were the 'brothers of Jesus' mentioned in the Gospels?
topics.answer: The Catholic Church teaches that Mary remained a perpetual virgin before, during, and after the birth of Jesus. The 'brothers of Jesus' mentioned in the Gospels (Mk 6:3) refer to cousins or close kinsmen — the common usage of the Greek word 'adelphos' in a Semitic cultural context where no separate word for 'cousin' existed. Evidence: (1) The same 'brothers' — James and Joses — are identified in Matthew 27:56 as sons of 'the other Mary,' not of Mary the mother of Jesus. (2) On the cross, Jesus entrusted his mother to John (Jn 19:26-27) — he would not have done this if Mary had other children to care for her. (3) Luke 1:34 — Mary's question 'How can this be, since I have no relations with a man?' implies a vow of virginity already made before the angel spoke. (4) Ezekiel 44:2 prophetically describes a gate that remains shut because the Lord God of Israel passed through it — a type of Mary's perpetual virginity.

scripture.text: But Mary said to the angel, 'How can this be, since I have no relations with a man?'

When Jesus saw his mother and the disciple there whom he loved, he said to his mother, 'Woman, behold, your son.' Then he said to the disciple, 'Behold, your mother.' And from that hour the disciple took her into his own home.
This gate must remain closed; it must not be opened, no one may enter through it. Since the LORD, the God of Israel, has entered through it, it must remain closed.

churchFathers.author: St. Jerome
churchFathers.quote: Mary, of whom Christ was born, in her virginity had no equal. She is virgin before the birth, virgin in the birth, and virgin after the birth.

topics.title: Sacred Images and Statues
topics.question: Does using statues and images in Catholic worship violate the commandment against idolatry?
topics.answer: The Catholic use of sacred images is not idolatry. Idolatry means worshipping a created thing as God. Catholics do not worship statues — they venerate them as reminders of holy persons, just as we keep photos of loved ones. God himself commanded the making of religious images: He ordered the golden Cherubim on the Ark of the Covenant (Exo 25:18-20), the bronze serpent on a pole (Num 21:8-9), and twelve bronze oxen supporting the bronze sea in the Temple (1 Kgs 7:25). The prohibition in Exodus 20:4 is against making images to worship as gods, not against images per se. When Israel began worshipping the bronze serpent, Hezekiah destroyed it (2 Kgs 18:4) — not because it was an image, but because they were treating it as a deity. Catholics use images to aid devotion and recall the holy persons they represent. We honor the person, not the material object. This is no different from saluting a flag — you honor the country the flag represents, not the cloth itself.

scripture.text: Make two cherubim of hammered gold for the two ends of the cover. Make one cherub at one end, and the other at the other end, of one piece with the cover, at each end. The cherubim shall have their wings spread out above, sheltering the cover with their wings, facing each other, with their faces looking toward the cover.

The LORD said to Moses: Make a seraph and mount it on a pole, and everyone who has been bitten will look at it and recover. Accordingly Moses made a bronze serpent and mounted it on a pole, and whenever the serpent bit someone, the person looked at the bronze serpent and recovered.

churchFathers.author: St. John of Damascus
churchFathers.quote: I do not worship matter; I worship the Creator of matter who became matter for my sake and deigned to dwell in matter, who worked out my salvation through matter.

topics.title: Asking Saints to Intercede
topics.question: Why do Catholics pray to saints? Is this not forbidden since there is only one mediator, Jesus Christ?
topics.answer: Catholics do not worship saints — they ask them to pray on our behalf, just as we ask living friends to pray for us. The objection about 'one mediator' (1 Tim 2:5) misses the point: Paul writes this in the same passage where he urges Christians to pray for one another (1 Tim 2:1). If asking others to pray for you violated Christ's unique mediation, then asking a pastor or friend to pray for you would also be wrong. The saints in heaven are alive in God (Lk 20:38) and more powerful in intercession than anyone on earth. Revelation shows the saints and angels in heaven presenting our prayers to God like incense (Rev 5:8, Rev 8:3-4). Mary's intercession at Cana moved Jesus to act (Jn 2:3-11). The communion of saints means we remain united in prayer — those in heaven, those on earth, and those in purgatory — as one family of God.

scripture.text: When he took it, the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down before the Lamb. Each of the elders held a harp and gold bowls filled with incense, which are the prayers of the holy ones.

First of all, then, I ask that supplications, prayers, petitions, and thanksgivings be offered for everyone, for kings and for all in authority, that we may lead a quiet and tranquil life in all devotion and dignity. This is good and pleasing to God our savior.
He is not God of the dead, but of the living, for to him all are alive.

churchFathers.author: Origen of Alexandria
churchFathers.quote: But not the high priest alone prays for those who pray sincerely, but also the angels... and also the souls of the saints who have already fallen asleep.

topics.title: Purgatory
topics.question: Is purgatory found in Scripture, and why do Catholics believe in it?
topics.answer: Purgatory is the state of purification for those who die in God's friendship but still need cleansing before entering heaven. Nothing unclean can enter heaven (Rev 21:27). It follows logically that a person who dies with minor sins or imperfectly repented sins cannot go straight to heaven — yet they are not lost. Paul describes being saved 'as through fire,' with incomplete works burned away (1 Cor 3:15). Jesus speaks of sins forgiven 'in the age to come' (Mat 12:32) — implying a process after death. The Book of Maccabees records Judas Maccabeus offering prayers and sacrifices for soldiers who died in sin (2 Mac 12:43-46) — and the text calls this a 'holy and devout thought.' The ancient Jewish practice of praying for the dead confirms this understanding. Purgatory is not a second chance for the damned — only those already saved pass through it. It is God's mercy and our greatest hope: that even our imperfections do not keep us from Him.

scripture.text: But if someone's work is burned up, that one will suffer loss; the person will be saved, but only as through fire.
And whoever speaks a word against the Son of Man will be forgiven; but whoever speaks against the holy Spirit will not be forgiven, either in this age or in the age to come.
Thus he made atonement for the dead that they might be freed from this sin.
Nothing unclean will enter it, nor anyone who does abominable things or tells lies. Only those will enter whose names are written in the Lamb's book of life.

churchFathers.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
churchFathers.quote: That there should be some fire even after this life is not incredible, and it can be inquired into and either be discovered or left hidden whether some of the faithful may be saved, some more slowly and some more quickly in the greater or lesser degree in which they loved the good things that perish.

topics.title: The Primacy of Peter and the Papacy
topics.question: Did Jesus give Peter a unique leadership role, and does the Pope have authority over the Church today?
topics.answer: Jesus gave Peter a unique role that he did not give to any other apostle. He renamed him 'Cephas' (Rock) — a name never before given to anyone — and built his Church on this rock (Mat 16:18). He gave Peter the 'keys of the kingdom,' symbolizing supreme authority (Mat 16:19), exactly as a king's prime minister held 'the key of the house of David' (Isa 22:22). He commissioned Peter to 'strengthen your brothers' (Lk 22:32) and to 'feed my sheep' (Jn 21:15-17). In Acts, Peter is consistently the spokesman and leader of the apostolic community. The bishops of Rome, as successors of Peter, inherited this unique authority. This is not merely a Catholic claim — early Church Fathers from the first and second centuries recognized the special position of the Roman bishop. The papacy ensures unity of faith and protects the Church from error in defining doctrines of faith and morals.

scripture.text: And so I say to you, you are Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church, and the gates of the netherworld shall not prevail against it. I will give you the keys to the kingdom of heaven. Whatever you bind on earth shall be bound in heaven; and whatever you loose on earth shall be loosed in heaven.
Simon, Simon, behold Satan has demanded to sift all of you like wheat, but I have prayed that your own faith may not fail; and once you have turned back, you must strengthen your brothers.

When they had finished breakfast, Jesus said to Simon Peter, 'Simon, son of John, do you love me more than these?' He said to him, 'Yes, Lord, you know that I love you.' He said to him, 'Feed my lambs.'

churchFathers.author: St. Clement of Rome
churchFathers.quote: If anyone disobey the things which have been said by Him through us, let them know that they will involve themselves in transgression and in no small danger.

churchFathers.author: St. Irenaeus of Lyon
churchFathers.quote: For it is a matter of necessity that every church should agree with this church, on account of its preeminent authority.

topics.title: Indulgences
topics.question: What exactly is an indulgence, and did the Church ever sell them?
topics.answer: An indulgence is the remission of the temporal punishment due to already-forgiven sins. Sin has two consequences: guilt (removed in confession) and temporal punishment (satisfied through penance, suffering, or purgatory). An indulgence draws on the 'treasury of merit' — the superabundant merits of Christ and the saints — and applies them to remove temporal punishment, either for oneself or for a soul in purgatory. The basis is the binding-and-loosing authority given to the Church (Mat 16:19, Mat 18:18). The historical abuse of selling indulgences was a corruption condemned by the Church itself — the Council of Trent (1563) explicitly forbade any exchange of money for indulgences. The abuse was real, but it does not invalidate the doctrine, just as corrupt banking does not make money itself evil. Today, indulgences are obtained through prayer, pilgrimage, acts of charity, and reception of sacraments — never for money.

scripture.text: I will give you the keys to the kingdom of heaven. Whatever you bind on earth shall be bound in heaven; and whatever you loose on earth shall be loosed in heaven.
Whomever you forgive anything, so do I. For indeed what I have forgiven, if I have forgiven anything, has been for you in the presence of Christ.
Now I rejoice in my sufferings for your sake, and in my flesh I am filling up what is lacking in the afflictions of Christ on behalf of his body, which is the church.

churchFathers.quote: If, because of the shortness of life, some people do not make full atonement, peace is granted them, so that they may be aided by the full atonement of others.

topics.title: Infant Baptism
topics.question: Is baptizing infants biblical, or should baptism only be given to believing adults?
topics.answer: Infant baptism has been practiced since apostolic times and is well grounded in Scripture. In the New Testament, entire households were baptized when the head of household believed — and households included infants and children (Acts 16:15, Acts 16:33, 1 Cor 1:16). Paul explicitly connects baptism to circumcision (Col 2:11-12), and circumcision was performed on 8-day-old infants in the Old Covenant — the New Covenant sacrament that replaces circumcision therefore also applies to infants. Jesus declared that children belong to the Kingdom of God (Mk 10:14) and warned against preventing them from coming to him. Original sin is inherited, not chosen — infants need salvation just as adults do. Since baptism is the 'door to salvation' (CCC 1213), withholding it from infants is withholding grace from the most vulnerable. The early Church universally baptized infants: Origen (c. 184–253) writes that 'the Church received from the apostles the tradition of baptizing infants.'

scripture.text: He took them in at that hour of the night and bathed their wounds; then he and all his family were baptized at once.

In him you were also circumcised with a circumcision not administered by hand, by stripping off the carnal body, with the circumcision of Christ. You were buried with him in baptism, in which you were also raised with him through faith in the power of God, who raised him from the dead.
When Jesus saw this he became indignant and said to them, 'Let the children come to me; do not prevent them, for the kingdom of God belongs to such as these.'

churchFathers.author: Origen of Alexandria
churchFathers.quote: The Church received from the apostles the tradition of giving baptism even to infants. For the apostles, to whom were committed the secrets of divine mysteries, knew that there is in everyone the innate stains of sin, which must be washed away through water and the Spirit.

topics.title: The Sacrament of Confession
topics.question: Why do Catholics confess sins to a priest? Can we not confess directly to God?
topics.answer: We can and should always speak to God about our sins. But the sacrament of Confession is Christ's own gift for the certain forgiveness of sins committed after baptism. On Easter Sunday, the Risen Christ breathed on the apostles and said: 'Receive the holy Spirit. Whose sins you forgive are forgiven them, and whose sins you retain are retained' (Jn 20:22-23). This authority to forgive or retain sins is meaningless unless a priest actually hears confessed sins — how can he discern what to forgive if he does not know what was done? James commands us to 'confess your sins to one another' (Jas 5:16). The priest does not forgive by his own power — he acts as Christ's instrument. The sacrament provides three gifts unavailable in private prayer: absolute certainty of forgiveness, grace to resist the same sin in the future, and authoritative spiritual direction. This visible sign of invisible grace is exactly what incarnational religion — God becoming human — looks like in practice.

scripture.text: Jesus said to them again, 'Peace be with you. As the Father has sent me, so I send you.' And when he had said this, he breathed on them and said to them, 'Receive the holy Spirit. Whose sins you forgive are forgiven them, and whose sins you retain are retained.'
Therefore, confess your sins to one another and pray for one another, that you may be healed.

And all this is from God, who has reconciled us to himself through Christ and given us the ministry of reconciliation... So we are ambassadors for Christ, as if God were appealing through us. We implore you on behalf of Christ, be reconciled to God.

churchFathers.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
churchFathers.quote: Of how much greater faith and salutary fear are they who do not defer confessing their sins to the priests of God, but confess with a humble and simple confession.

topics.title: The Real Presence in the Holy Eucharist
topics.question: Are the bread and wine at Mass truly the Body and Blood of Christ, or are they just symbols?
topics.answer: Catholics believe — as Christians did for the first fifteen centuries — that the bread and wine at Mass truly become the Body and Blood of Christ. This is not a symbol. Jesus said 'This is my body' and 'This is my blood' using the Greek word 'sarx' (real flesh), not 'semeion' (sign). In John 6, when Jesus said 'Unless you eat my flesh and drink my blood you have no life in you' (Jn 6:53), many disciples left — and Jesus did not call them back to explain he was only speaking symbolically. Paul warns that eating the bread or drinking the cup 'unworthily' makes one guilty of 'the body and blood of the Lord' (1 Cor 11:27) — a warning absurd if these were mere symbols. 1 Corinthians 10:16 calls the cup 'a participation in the blood of Christ.' The early Church — St. Ignatius, St. Justin, St. Irenaeus — all wrote explicitly that the Eucharist is the real flesh and blood of Christ. The change of substance while appearances remain is called transubstantiation.

scripture.text: Jesus said to them, 'Amen, amen, I say to you, unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you do not have life within you. Whoever eats my flesh and drinks my blood has eternal life, and I will raise him on the last day. For my flesh is true food, and my blood is true drink. Whoever eats my flesh and drinks my blood remains in me and I in him.'
The cup of blessing that we bless, is it not a participation in the blood of Christ? The bread that we break, is it not a participation in the body of Christ?

Then he took the bread, said the blessing, broke it, and gave it to them, saying, 'This is my body, which will be given for you; do this in memory of me.' And likewise the cup after they had eaten, saying, 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood, which will be shed for you.'

churchFathers.author: St. Ignatius of Antioch
churchFathers.quote: They abstain from the Eucharist and from prayer because they do not confess that the Eucharist is the flesh of our Savior Jesus Christ, flesh which suffered for our sins and which the Father, in his goodness, raised up again.

churchFathers.author: St. Justin Martyr
churchFathers.quote: For not as common bread and common drink do we receive these; but... the food which is blessed by the prayer of His word is the flesh and blood of that Jesus who was made flesh.

topics.title: The Sacrament of Holy Orders
topics.question: Is the Catholic priesthood biblical, or was it invented by the Church? Why can priests not marry?
topics.answer: The New Testament priesthood is not a continuation of the Levitical priesthood but a participation in Christ's own eternal priesthood (Heb 7:24). Christ chose twelve apostles, gave them unique authority to teach (Mat 28:18-20), forgive sins (Jn 20:22-23), judge (Mat 18:17-18), and consecrate the Eucharist (Lk 22:19-20). He gave them authority to ordain others — this is demonstrated in Acts 6:3-6 (the seven deacons ordained by apostles) and in Paul's letters to Timothy and Titus. Paul explicitly says he was made a priest to offer the sacrifice of the Gentiles (Rom 15:16). The laying on of hands passes this sacred power in unbroken succession. Regarding celibacy: the requirement that bishops be 'husband of one wife' (1 Tim 3:2) refers to being faithful in marriage, not to mandatory marriage. Paul himself was celibate and recommended it for total dedication to God (1 Cor 7:32-34). Jesus acknowledged that some renounce marriage 'for the sake of the kingdom of heaven' (Mat 19:12). Priestly celibacy is not a doctrinal requirement but a disciplinary one in the Latin Rite, rooted in apostolic example.

scripture.text: But he, because he remains forever, has a priesthood that does not pass away.

Brothers, select from among you seven reputable men, filled with the Spirit and wisdom, whom we shall appoint to this task... They presented these men to the apostles who prayed and laid hands on them.
For this reason, I remind you to stir into flame the gift of God that you have through the imposition of my hands.

churchFathers.quote: The bishop presides in the place of God, while the presbyters function as the council of the Apostles, and the deacons, who are most dear to me, are entrusted with the ministry of Jesus Christ.

churchFathers.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
churchFathers.quote: The bishops are the successors of the apostles, bound to them by an unbroken chain of ordination.

topics.title: The Holy Mass as a Sacrifice
topics.question: Is the Mass a re-crucifixion of Christ? Hebrews says Christ died once for all — why repeat the sacrifice?
topics.answer: The Mass does not re-crucify Christ or repeat his sacrifice. Christ died 'once for all' (Heb 9:26) — that one sacrifice is eternally sufficient. The Mass is the sacramental re-presentation (making present again) of that one sacrifice — not a new sacrifice, but the same sacrifice made accessible across time. At the Last Supper, Jesus said 'Do this in memory of me' — in Greek, 'anamnesis' does not mean merely to recall, but to make present again. He instituted the Mass as the ongoing ritual making his once-and-for-all sacrifice present to every generation. The daily offerings of animal blood in the Old Testament could not take away sins (Heb 10:4, 11) — but the priestly sacrifice of Christ's own body and blood on the cross can. This sacrifice was foretold in Malachi 1:11: 'From the rising of the sun to its setting, my name is great among the nations; everywhere incense is offered to my name and a pure offering.' Only the Mass fulfills this worldwide, daily sacrifice to God. Through Mass, Christ offers himself to the Father through the ministry of the priest.
